name: Naturepedia Carbon & Microbial Life Map
description: Machine-readable relationship map for the Naturepedia Carbon & Microbial Life™ family.
architecture: Carbon & Microbial Life → Microbial Decomposition → Microbial Respiration → Microbial Biomass → Microbial Necromass → Microbial Carbon Pump → Carbon Mineralization → Carbon Stabilization by Microbes → Microbial Nutrient Cycling → Microbial Climate Feedbacks

systemNodes.name: Carbon & Microbial Life™
systemNodes.role: Parent system integrating microbial ecology with carbon transformation, storage, and climate regulation.

systemNodes.name: Microbial Decomposition
systemNodes.role: Breakdown of organic matter into simpler carbon compounds by bacteria, fungi, archaea, and soil food webs.

systemNodes.name: Microbial Respiration
systemNodes.role: Release of carbon dioxide through microbial metabolism and biological activity.

systemNodes.name: Microbial Biomass
systemNodes.role: Living microbial carbon pool responsible for nutrient transformation and ecosystem productivity.

systemNodes.name: Microbial Necromass
systemNodes.role: Dead microbial residues contributing to long-term soil organic matter and carbon storage.

systemNodes.name: Microbial Carbon Pump
systemNodes.role: Transformation of microbial biomass into persistent mineral-associated organic matter and stable soil carbon.

systemNodes.name: Carbon Mineralization
systemNodes.role: Conversion of organic carbon into inorganic forms through microbial metabolism.

systemNodes.name: Carbon Stabilization by Microbes
systemNodes.role: Microbial contribution to aggregate formation, humus production, mineral association, and persistent carbon storage.

systemNodes.name: Microbial Nutrient Cycling
systemNodes.role: Integration of microbial processes driving nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, and carbon cycling.

systemNodes.name: Microbial Climate Feedbacks
systemNodes.role: Microbial influence on atmospheric carbon dioxide, methane, decomposition rates, and long-term climate regulation.

relatedSystems.relationship: Provides the global carbon flows transformed by microbial metabolism.
Receives stabilized microbial carbon through necromass, aggregates, and mineral-associated organic matter.
Provides the physical and biological environment supporting microbial communities.
Provides the organisms responsible for carbon transformation and nutrient cycling.
Supplies root exudates and carbon substrates that sustain microbial communities.
Regulates soil moisture, oxygen availability, and microbial metabolic activity.
Links microbial decomposition to forest litter, woody biomass, and long-term ecosystem carbon storage.
Connects microbial respiration, decomposition, methane production, and greenhouse gas dynamics.
Links microbial processes with biodiversity, nutrient availability, resilience, and ecosystem stability.
